title: Reading and Use of English - Part 5
description: You are going to read an article about the media, journalism, and the changing information landscape. For questions 31 – 36, choose the answer (A, B, C or D) which you think fits best according to the text. Mark your answers on the separate answer sheet.

content.title: The News We Choose
content.subtitle: An article about journalism, media trust, and the challenge of the digital age.
content.text:
There has never been more information available, and there has rarely been a greater sense of uncertainty about what to believe. These two facts are connected. The internet, which was initially celebrated as a democratic revolution in the distribution of knowledge, has also created the conditions in which misinformation spreads at unprecedented speed, and in which the algorithms that curate our information diets are optimised for engagement rather than accuracy or balance. [31] The result is a paradox that sits at the heart of contemporary life: an abundance of content and a deficit of shared reality. [/31]

Journalism, in its traditional sense, was designed as a partial solution to this problem — a professional practice with its own standards of verification, fairness, and accountability. At its best, it still serves this function. Investigative reporting continues to expose corruption, hold governments to account, and surface stories that powerful interests would prefer to suppress. [32] At its worst, however, journalism has itself contributed to the problem it was meant to solve — through sensationalism, political partisanship, financial conflicts of interest, and a willingness to prioritise speed over accuracy. [/32]

The economic model that sustained professional journalism for over a century has collapsed. Classified advertising — once a reliable revenue stream for local newspapers — migrated online. Display advertising followed. The result has been a devastating contraction in the newspaper industry, with local journalism particularly hard hit. [33] When local news organisations disappear, the communities they served lose not only a source of information but a mechanism for civic accountability — and research has shown that their absence correlates with reduced voter participation and increased political polarisation. [/33]

Digital platforms have stepped, at least partially, into the space vacated by traditional media. But they are not journalistic organisations in any meaningful sense. They do not employ reporters, conduct investigations, or bear editorial responsibility for the content they distribute. [34] Their role is more that of a postal service than a publisher — yet the power they exercise over what is seen, and by whom, vastly exceeds that of any traditional media organisation. [/34] Efforts to regulate them have been politically fraught and largely unsuccessful.

Media literacy — the ability to evaluate sources, identify bias, and think critically about the information we consume — has become an increasingly urgent educational priority. [35] Studies consistently show that people systematically overestimate their ability to identify unreliable information, a phenomenon sometimes called the 'illusion of media literacy'. The practical implication is that teaching people to be sceptical is necessary but not sufficient; they must also develop the specific skills to evaluate evidence and understand how information is produced. [/35]

Despite the challenges, journalism retains a capacity to make a difference — to shift public opinion, trigger official investigations, and occasionally change history. [36] The conditions in which it operates have become more difficult, but the underlying need for accurate, accountable reporting on public affairs has not diminished. If anything, in a world drowning in unverified claims, the value of careful, sceptical journalism has never been greater. [/36]

questions.question: What 'paradox' does the author identify in the opening paragraph?
options: A) There is too much information for journalists to be able to report everything.
B) Despite unprecedented access to information, agreement on facts has decreased.
C) The internet provides reliable information to some people but not to others.
D) Algorithms make it easier to find information but harder to find entertainment.
questions.explanation: The paradox is described as 'an abundance of content and a deficit of shared reality' — more information is available, but less agreement on what is true.

questions.question: The author's view of journalism is best described as
options: A) balanced, acknowledging both its best and worst qualities.
B) nostalgic, suggesting that journalism was better in the past.
C) entirely negative, as it has contributed more to the problem than the solution.
D) largely positive, with some recognition of its failures.
questions.explanation: The author describes journalism 'at its best' as serving a vital function and 'at its worst' as contributing to the problems it should solve, presenting a balanced view.

questions.question: The disappearance of local news organisations is described as harmful because
options: A) it reduces citizens' access to national and international news.
B) it removes competition that keeps national news organisations honest.
C) it undermines democratic participation and increases political division.
D) it eliminates jobs in communities that are already economically deprived.
questions.explanation: Research shows that the absence of local news 'correlates with reduced voter participation and increased political polarisation', highlighting damage to democratic life.

questions.question: The author compares digital platforms to 'a postal service' in order to highlight
options: A) the speed with which they can distribute information to large audiences.
B) the difficulty of holding them legally responsible for the content they carry.
C) the fact that they make money by delivering content rather than producing it.
D) the contrast between the influence they wield and their lack of editorial responsibility.
questions.explanation: A postal service simply delivers without responsibility for content — the comparison highlights that platforms 'exercise vast power' over what is seen despite bearing 'no editorial responsibility'.

questions.question: What does the author mean by the 'illusion of media literacy'?
options: A) Media literacy courses create false confidence rather than real skills.
B) People tend to think they are better at detecting unreliable information than they are.
C) The media deliberately creates an impression of reliability that is misleading.
D) Most people believe the media is less reliable than it actually is.
questions.explanation: The phenomenon describes how 'people systematically overestimate their ability to identify unreliable information' — they believe they are more media literate than they actually are.

questions.question: The author ends the article with the suggestion that
options: A) good journalism is now more valuable than at any previous point.
B) journalism will soon recover its former economic strength.
C) the problems facing journalism are too great to be resolved.
D) digital platforms will eventually replace traditional journalism entirely.
questions.explanation: The author concludes that 'in a world drowning in unverified claims, the value of careful, sceptical journalism has never been greater', indicating journalism is more needed than ever.
